title: AI History — Paradigms and What Comes Next
key_insight: AI history is driven by paradigm shifts, not incremental progress. Each new paradigm obsoletes the previous one — and renders massive prior investments worthless.
body:
## The Paradigm Sequence

```
Paradigm 1: Symbolic AI (1956–1980s)
  Core idea: intelligence = logic + symbol manipulation
  Tool:      expert systems, Prolog, theorem provers
  Failure:   brittleness — rules can't cover the real world

Paradigm 2: Statistical ML (1990s–2010s)
  Core idea: intelligence = patterns in data + Bayesian inference
  Tool:      SVMs, random forests, logistic regression
  Success:   robust on structured data
  Failure:   requires feature engineering, doesn't scale to raw data

Paradigm 3: Deep Learning (2012–present)
  Core idea: intelligence = hierarchical representations from raw data
  Tool:      CNNs, RNNs, Transformers
  Success:   superhuman on vision, language, games
  Open Q:    data efficiency, reasoning, long-horizon planning

Paradigm 4: Foundation Models + Prompting (2020–present)
  Core idea: one large model, many downstream tasks via prompting
  Tool:      GPT-4, Claude, Llama, CLIP
  Impact:    redefines what 'building AI' means
```

## The Benchmark Story

Every paradigm change produced a signature benchmark result:

| Year | Benchmark | Method | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1997 | Chess (ELO) | Minimax + α-β | Beats Kasparov |
| 2012 | ImageNet | AlexNet (CNN) | 10.9pp drop in error |
| 2016 | Go | AlphaGo (MCTS + RL) | Beats Lee Sedol |
| 2018 | GLUE (NLP) | BERT | Matches human baseline |
| 2021 | Protein folding | AlphaFold2 | Solves 50-year problem |
| 2023 | Bar Exam, SAT | GPT-4 | 90th percentile |

## The Compute Curve (and Its Implications)

```python
# AI compute has doubled every 3.4 months since 2012 (OpenAI 2018 analysis)
import math
years_since_2012 = 12
doublings = 12 * 12 / 3.4  # ~42 doublings
compute_increase = 2 ** 42  # ~4 trillion times more compute

# This is not sustainable — we are running out of training data at current rates
# Implications:
# - Synthetic data generation
# - More efficient architectures
# - Test-time compute (inference scaling)
```

## What's Actually Hard (Remaining Open Problems)

- **Reasoning and planning**: LLMs fail on multi-step reasoning without scaffolding
- **Sample efficiency**: humans learn from few examples, LLMs need billions
- **Physical world grounding**: no model truly understands causality
- **Long-term memory**: context windows are finite
- **Alignment**: optimising objectives that truly match human values
common_mistakes: Assuming current capabilities will scale indefinitely — every paradigm has eventually hit diminishing returns
Dismissing symbolic AI entirely — neurosymbolic approaches combining deep learning with logic are a serious research direction
Confusing benchmark performance with real-world capability — GPT-4 scores 90th percentile on bar exam but makes basic factual errors
mini_exercise: Research 'AlphaGo vs AlphaGo Zero'. AlphaGo was trained on human games. AlphaGo Zero started from scratch with only self-play. Which was stronger? What does this imply about the value of human knowledge vs raw computation?
real_world_usage: DeepMind's AlphaFold2 cracked the protein folding problem in 2021 — a 50-year biology grand challenge. It did so using an architecture that mixed multiple AI paradigms: multiple sequence alignment (classical bioinformatics) + attention (Transformers) + equivariant networks (geometric deep learning). Real breakthroughs often combine paradigms.
explain_back_prompt: Describe the four paradigms in AI history. For each, identify: what was the core idea? Why did it succeed? What were its limits? Which problems is the current paradigm still failing on?
